message:
chore: stage concurrent agent changes (beads, realdb_e2e, perf_runner)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

@@ -357,7 +357,7 @@
 {"id":"bd-29vi","title":"§7.1 SQLite Native WAL Checksum Algorithm","description":"STATUS: CLOSED (superseded rollup)

This bead is a consolidated/rolled-up version of §7.1 created during an earlier spec-to-beads conversion pass.

DO NOT implement from this rollup directly.

Use this replacement bead instead (plan-of-record):
- bd-30b5 — §7.1-7.3 Checksum Algorithms: SQLite Native + XXH3 + CRC-32C + Three-Tier Hash Strategy

NOTE: The original consolidated spec extract for this rollup lives in the bead comments.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T04:58:58.957615237Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-08T17:32:53.326962526Z","closed_at":"2026-02-08T06:25:12.550358386Z","close_reason":"Content merged into bd-30b5 (P1 §7.1-7.3)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-29vi","depends_on_id":"bd-1nk","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T06:09:40.055663840Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":5,"issue_id":"bd-29vi","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## §7.1 SQLite Native Checksum Algorithm

WAL uses custom 64-bit checksum (two u32 accumulators) for frame integrity. Must be implemented exactly for file format compatibility.

**Algorithm (from wal.c):**
```rust
pub fn wal_checksum(data: &[u8], s1_init: u32, s2_init: u32, big_end_cksum: bool) -> (u32, u32) {
    assert!(data.len() % 8 == 0);
    let native_cksum = big_end_cksum == cfg!(target_endian = "big");
    for chunk in data.chunks_exact(8) {
        let (a, b) = if native_cksum {
            // nativeCksum=1: read u32 in native byte order (no swap)
            (u32::from_ne_bytes([chunk[0..4]]), u32::from_ne_bytes([chunk[4..8]]))
        } else {
            // nativeCksum=0: BYTESWAP32 each u32 before accumulating
            (u32::from_ne_bytes([chunk[3],chunk[2],chunk[1],chunk[0]]),
             u32::from_ne_bytes([chunk[7],chunk[6],chunk[5],chunk[4]]))
        };
        s1 = s1.wrapping_add(a).wrapping_add(s2);
        s2 = s2.wrapping_add(b).wrapping_add(s1);
    }
    (s1, s2)
}
```

**CRITICAL clarification:** s1 updated with FIRST u32 word, s2 with SECOND u32 word per 8-byte chunk. Incorrect transcriptions "avalanche" both words into both accumulators — breaks binary interop.

**Endianness from WAL magic:**
- 0x377f0682 (bit 0=0): bigEndCksum=0 (little-endian creator). On LE reader: nativeCksum=1 (no swap). On BE reader: nativeCksum=0 (swap).
- 0x377f0683 (bit 0=1): bigEndCksum=1 (big-endian creator). On BE reader: nativeCksum=1. On LE reader: nativeCksum=0.
- Magic always read via big-endian u32 decoding (matches sqlite3Get4byte).
- FrankenSQLite writes WAL using native byte order for performance.

**Cumulative chaining:** Each frame's checksum chains from previous:
- WAL header: (hdr_cksum1, hdr_cksum2) = wal_checksum(header[0..24], 0, 0, big_end_cksum)
- Frame 0: wal_checksum(frame0_hdr[0..8] ++ page0_data, hdr_cksum1, hdr_cksum2, ...)
- Frame N: wal_checksum(frameN_hdr[0..8] ++ pageN_data, s1_{N-1}, s2_{N-1}, ...)

Hash chain: modifying any frame invalidates all subsequent checksums, detecting corruption and truncation.
","created_at":"2026-02-08T04:58:59Z"},{"id":474,"issue_id":"bd-29vi","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Closed as duplicate of bd-30b5 (§7.1-7.3 Checksum Algorithms). Content merged into bd-30b5 comment 109.","created_at":"2026-02-08T07:43:52Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2a3y","title":"Replace MemPageStore with TransactionPageIo in production cursor path","description":"## Goal

Replace the production cursor/page path that currently relies on `MemPageStore` with `TransactionPageIo`, so real execution flows through pager/WAL/MVCC.

## Scope

- Cursor reads/writes go through transaction-scoped page I/O
- No MemPageStore usage on the production path (tests may still use it explicitly)","acceptance_criteria":"Acceptance Criteria

- Production execution path no longer uses `MemPageStore` for cursor operations.
- A small end-to-end workload (DML + commit) uses TransactionPageIo and passes existing correctness tests.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:38:05.087742064Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T20:01:38.856661903Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T20:01:38.856643127Z","close_reason":"Storage cursors route via TransactionHandle; fixed pager commit OOM so e2e correctness_concurrent_writes passes.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2a3y","depends_on_id":"bd-1dqg","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:38:19.434159752Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2a3y","depends_on_id":"bd-25c6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:38:19.050204108Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-2a58","title":"ORDER BY rowid/oid/_rowid_ not supported in table-backed SELECT","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:36:41.094870566Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T17:37:00.368684869Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T17:37:00.368666144Z","close_reason":"Fixed: added SortKeySource enum and resolve_sort_key() to handle rowid/oid/_rowid_ in ORDER BY codegen. 2 new tests pass.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":1203,"issue_id":"bd-2a58","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Fix: ORDER BY rowid support in codegen

**Root cause:** `codegen_select_ordered_scan` resolved ORDER BY terms via
`resolve_column_ref`, which only looked up declared table columns. The implicit
`rowid` / `_rowid_` / `oid` pseudo-columns were not recognized, causing
`SELECT * FROM t ORDER BY rowid` to fail with "non-column ORDER BY expression".

The e2e comparison harness (`logical_dump_tables`) fell back to `ORDER BY 1`
(first column) instead of `ORDER BY rowid`, producing hash mismatches
between FrankenSQLite and C SQLite.

**Fix:**
- Added `is_rowid_alias()` helper recognizing `rowid`, `_rowid_`, `oid`
- Added `SortKeySource` enum with `Column(usize)` and `Rowid` variants
- Added `resolve_sort_key()` that returns either variant
- Updated `codegen_select_ordered_scan` to use `resolve_sort_key` and emit
  `Opcode::Rowid` for rowid sort keys instead of `Opcode::Column`
- Added 2 tests: `test_select_order_by_rowid` and `test_select_order_by_rowid_aliases`

**Files changed:**
- `crates/fsqlite-vdbe/src/codegen.rs` (new helpers + updated sort codegen)
- `crates/fsqlite-core/src/connection.rs` (2 new tests)

**Quality gates:** 675 core tests pass, full workspace clippy + fmt clean.
","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:37:00Z"}]}
-{"id":"bd-2als","title":"EPIC: RealDB Mega-Demos (Corpus + SHA256 + Concurrency Perf + Corruption/Recovery + TUI)","description":"## Why

We want **real, visceral end-to-end demos** that prove FrankenSQLite’s two flagship claims on *real SQLite databases*:

1. **Concurrent writers** (page-level MVCC) can deliver clear throughput/scaling wins versus stock SQLite’s single-writer bottleneck.
2. **Information-theoretic durability / repair** (WAL-FEC + “raptorq everything” mentality) can recover from corruption patterns where stock SQLite loses data or cannot open the DB.

This is not a toy benchmark. The point is to run against **actual `.sqlite` files** from `/dp` projects (Beads DBs, caches, metadata stores, etc.), apply **massively concurrent write workloads**, and produce evidence-rich artifacts.

## What We Are Building

A cohesive “RealDB” harness (implemented under `crates/fsqlite-e2e` + the `sample_sqlite_db_files/` corpus) that can:

- Discover and ingest real DBs into a **local-only** corpus under `sample_sqlite_db_files/golden/`.
- Run deterministic workloads against **working copies** (never originals; never mutate `golden/`).
- Execute the same workload against:
  - **C SQLite** (oracle baseline)
  - **FrankenSQLite** (`fsqlite`), optionally with MVCC/concurrent mode enabled
- Verify results under an explicit, tiered equivalence contract.
- Benchmark and report speedup with fairness controls (PRAGMAs, warmup/repeats, environment capture).
- Inject deterministic corruption into copies and run **baseline-loss vs recovery** comparisons.
- Provide a **frankentui**-powered UX (TUI) and narrative walkthroughs suitable for demos/video.

## Safety Invariants (Non-Negotiable)

- Never run anything destructive against `/dp/...` originals.
- `sample_sqlite_db_files/golden/` is immutable input.
- All corruption happens on fresh copies and must emit a proof bundle (before/after sha256, seed, scenario metadata).
- Never commit DB bytes from `/dp` to git; only metadata/manifests/checksums/docs are tracked.

## Correctness / Equivalence Contract

We do not pretend “equivalence” is one thing. The harness must clearly label and enforce:

- **Tier 1 (Raw byte identity):** final DB file sha256 matches (bit-for-bit). This is a hard target for specific “layout-deterministic” workloads and is our strongest compatibility signal.
- **Tier 2 (Canonical identity):** run a deterministic canonicalization pipeline (e.g., `VACUUM INTO` / stable PRAGMAs) and require canonical sha256 to match.
- **Tier 3 (Logical equivalence):** schema + stable deterministic dumps match (rows, indexes as needed), with good diagnostics.

## Performance Contract

- Same seeded workload generator, concurrency sweep, and operation mix across engines.
- Same fairness PRAGMAs and comparable durability mode (document any intentional differences).
- Report throughput, latency percentiles, retry/busy/abort rates, and speedup curves.

## Where This Lives

- Code: `crates/fsqlite-e2e/` binaries (`realdb-e2e`, `e2e-runner`, `corruption-demo`, TUI viewers).
- Corpus: `sample_sqlite_db_files/` (golden/working/metadata/checksums/manifests).

## Relationship To Other Epics

This epic is the canonical tracker for the RealDB mega-demo effort (corpus + correctness + perf + corruption/recovery + UX + CI). Any alternate/older RealDB plan epics should be treated as deprecated once their useful notes are migrated here.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- `cargo run -p fsqlite-e2e --bin e2e-runner -- run-smoke` passes locally on a clean checkout.
- At least 5 real fixtures from `/dp` are ingested as immutable golden copies with metadata + checksums.
- At least one workload/fixture pair produces Tier-1 (raw sha256) equality between sqlite3 and fsqlite (or Tier-2 canonical sha256 equality if Tier-1 is not expected).
- Benchmark report exists (JSONL + Markdown) showing scaling and speedup vs sqlite3 on at least one real fixture.
- At least one corruption scenario demonstrates a clear “sqlite3 loses / fsqlite recovers” story with a proof bundle.
- A TUI can browse fixtures and run/view results (at minimum: list fixtures + open a run report).
- CI smoke job exists that runs a small RealDB subset and uploads artifacts for regression tracking.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:20:38.857344970Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T17:37:32.230225497Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["bench","corruption","demo","e2e","realdb","recovery","tui"]}

- **Tier 2 (Canonical identity):** run a deterministic canonicalization pipeline (e.g., `VACUUM INTO` / stable PRAGMAs) and require canonical sha256 to match.
- **Tier 3 (Logical equivalence):** schema + stable deterministic dumps match (rows, indexes as needed), with good diagnostics.

## Relationship To Other Epics

This epic is the canonical tracker for the RealDB mega-demo effort (corpus + correctness + perf + corruption/recovery + UX + CI). Any alternate/older RealDB plan epics should be treated as deprecated once their useful notes are migrated here.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- `cargo run -p fsqlite-e2e --bin e2e-runner -- run-smoke` passes locally on a clean checkout.
- At least 5 real fixtures from `/dp` are ingested as immutable golden copies with metadata + checksums.
- At least one workload/fixture pair produces Tier-1 (raw sha256) equality between sqlite3 and fsqlite (or Tier-2 canonical sha256 equality if Tier-1 is not expected).
- Benchmark report exists (JSONL + Markdown) showing scaling and speedup vs sqlite3 on at least one real fixture.
- At least one corruption scenario demonstrates a clear “sqlite3 loses / fsqlite recovers” story with a proof bundle.
- A TUI can browse fixtures and run/view results (at minimum: list fixtures + open a run report).
- CI smoke job exists that runs a small RealDB subset and uploads artifacts for regression tracking.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:20:38.857344970Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T21:09:12.354571997Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["bench","corruption","demo","e2e","realdb","recovery","tui"]}
 {"id":"bd-2als.1","title":"EPIC: Corpus + Fixture Ingestion (Safe Backup, Metadata, Manifest)","description":"## Goal

Make `sample_sqlite_db_files/` a **safe, reproducible, self-describing** local corpus of real SQLite databases that we can use for correctness/perf/corruption demos.

This epic is about **inputs**: which DBs we ingest, how we snapshot them safely from `/dp`, and how we ensure the bytes never drift.

## Core Safety Rules

- Never run workloads against `/dp/...` originals.
- Golden fixtures (`sample_sqlite_db_files/golden/`) are immutable.
- All test/demo runs operate on per-run working copies under `working/` (or per-run scratch dirs).
- Never commit golden DB bytes; only metadata/manifests/checksums are tracked.

## What “Import” Means

Import is **not `cp`**. Some `/dp` DBs have live WAL/SHM state. Import must take a consistent snapshot using the SQLite backup API (`sqlite3 ... ".backup 'dst'"` or equivalent via a library), then run `PRAGMA integrity_check` on the snapshot.

## Outputs

- `realdb-e2e corpus scan`:
  - deterministic discovery output (stable JSON)
  - filtering (depth/size/header validation/ignore patterns)
- `realdb-e2e corpus import`:
  - snapshot via backup API
  - provenance capture (source path, size, page_size, journal mode, etc.)
  - `integrity_check` proof
- Metadata + manifest + checksum discipline:
  - `checksums.sha256` for every file in `golden/`
  - `metadata/*.json` per fixture
  - (optional) `manifests/manifest.v1.json` validated against schema

## Why This Matters

If we don’t lock down golden inputs, every downstream result (correctness hashes, benchmarks, corruption proofs) becomes untrustworthy and non-reproducible.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- We can scan `/dp` and deterministically discover candidate `.sqlite`/`.db` files.
- We can import at least 5 fixtures via backup API, each passing `PRAGMA integrity_check`.
- `checksums.sha256` (tracked) matches on-disk golden files via `realdb-e2e corpus verify`.
- Metadata/manifest are sufficient to re-select fixtures by stable `db_id` with no tribal knowledge.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:20:56.279334314Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T20:12:13.651969136Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T20:12:13.651947676Z","close_reason":"Corpus pipeline is in place end-to-end: stable scan JSON + filters (bd-qaef), safe import via backup API + integrity_check + immutable checksums (bd-2als.1.1), FixtureMetadataV1 + page-size tagging + expanded fixtures (bd-2als.1.3/.1.2), verify is actionable and ignores sqlite sidecars/dotfiles (bd-i28l), manifest/metadata hardening closed.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","fixtures","manifest","realdb"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2als.1","depends_on_id":"bd-2als","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:20:56.279334314Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-2als.1.1","title":"Corpus Import CLI: safe snapshot (backup API) + integrity_check + strict checksums","description":"## Goal

Implement a **safe corpus import CLI** that snapshots real DBs from `/dp` into `sample_sqlite_db_files/golden/` without ever mutating originals.

This is the entry point that makes the entire RealDB harness possible.

## Requirements

Snapshot correctness:
- Use SQLite’s backup API (`sqlite3 <src> ".backup '<dst>'"` or equivalent) instead of `cp`.
- After snapshot, run `PRAGMA integrity_check;` on the *snapshot*.

Sidecars & journaling:
- Detect and record whether the source had `-wal` / `-shm` sidecars.
- Do not rely on copying sidecars as correctness; prefer backup API to materialize a consistent DB.

Provenance capture:
- Record:
  - `source_path` (absolute)
  - `db_id` (stable fixture identifier)
  - `golden_filename`
  - sha256 of the golden bytes
  - basic properties (page_size, user_version, application_id, journal_mode, freelist count, table count)

Corpus hygiene:
- Update `sample_sqlite_db_files/checksums.sha256` deterministically.
- Write per-fixture metadata JSON under `sample_sqlite_db_files/metadata/`.
- Never write into `golden/` except through this command.

## UX / Commands

Target flows:

- Import a single DB:
  `cargo run -p fsqlite-e2e --bin realdb-e2e -- corpus import --src /dp/foo/bar.db --db-id bar`

- Import from scan results:
  `realdb-e2e corpus scan --root /dp --max-depth 4 --json > scan.json`
  `realdb-e2e corpus import --from-scan scan.json --pick <db_id>`

(Exact flags are implementation-defined; the key is safety + determinism.)

## Pitfalls

- Live WAL state: a `cp` of `<db>` without its WAL is not a consistent snapshot.
- Secrets/PII: importing `/dp` DBs must be gated by an explicit inclusion policy (handled by metadata/redaction tasks).","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- Importing any candidate DB produces a golden copy that passes `PRAGMA integrity_check`.
- Import is implemented via backup API (not raw file copy).
- Import writes/updates `metadata/<db_id>.json` + `checksums.sha256` deterministically.
- The command refuses to operate on paths under `sample_sqlite_db_files/golden/` (no in-place overwrite).
- Errors are explicit and actionable (missing sqlite3, integrity_check fails, permission denied, etc.).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:13.431083048Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T17:39:22.494176835Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T17:35:23.911332493Z","close_reason":"Implemented safe corpus import: SQLite backup API snapshot -> golden/<id>.db, integrity_check on snapshot, strict sorted checksums (refuse overwrite), richer metadata (source_path, PRAGMAs, tables). Added unit/E2E tests in realdb-e2e.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","fixtures","manifest","realdb"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2als.1.1","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:13.431083048Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1202,"issue_id":"bd-2als.1.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## bd-2als.1.1 Completion: Corpus Import CLI

**Agent**: GentleBeaver (opus-4-6)

### Deliverables Verified

All three acceptance criteria from the bead title are met:

1. **Safe snapshot (backup API)**: `backup_sqlite_file()` uses `rusqlite::Connection::backup()` for consistent point-in-time snapshots — no raw file copies.

2. **integrity_check**: `sqlite_integrity_check()` runs `PRAGMA integrity_check` immediately after every capture and fails fast on any corruption.

3. **Strict checksums**: `upsert_checksum()` enforces immutability — refuses to overwrite an existing hash with a different value, maintains sorted order, and produces deterministic output.

### CLI Interface

`realdb-e2e corpus import` supports:
- `--db <path>` — source database (resolved via discovery if not a literal path)
- `--id <slug>` — stable fixture identifier
- `--tag <label>` — optional categorization
- `--golden-dir`, `--metadata-dir`, `--checksums` — configurable paths
- `--no-metadata` — skip metadata JSON generation
- `--allow-bad-header` — override SQLite magic header check

### Tests Added (16 new)

- `test_corpus_import_end_to_end` — full pipeline: backup → integrity → checksums → metadata
- `test_corpus_import_idempotent` — re-import succeeds without duplication
- `test_corpus_import_no_metadata_flag` — `--no-metadata` suppresses JSON
- `test_backup_sqlite_file_produces_valid_copy` — backup data fidelity
- `test_backup_passes_integrity_check` — backup passes PRAGMA integrity_check
- `test_sanitize_db_id_basic` / `_trims_underscores` / `_rejects_empty` — ID validation
- `test_upsert_checksum_creates_new_file` / `_idempotent` / `_refuses_hash_change` / `_maintains_sorted_order` — checksum immutability

### Quality Gates

- 32/32 tests pass in `realdb-e2e` binary
- Clippy clean (pedantic + nursery)
- No formatting diffs
","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:35:41Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-2als.1.2","title":"Corpus Expansion: ingest more /dp SQLite DBs + tag by size/journal/page_size/features","description":"## Goal

Grow the local RealDB fixture corpus by ingesting a diverse set of SQLite databases from `/dp` and other approved roots.

We want fixtures that represent:

- different sizes (tiny → mid → larger)
- different feature usage (FTS, triggers, indexes, many tables)
- different journal/page_size/encoding configurations
- realistic write patterns (beads DBs are great because they are write-heavy and concurrency-sensitive)

## Selection Criteria

Prefer:
- Beads databases (e.g., `.beads/beads.db` from various `/dp/*` repos)
- Anything with WAL usage (has or had `-wal`/`-shm`)
- Databases with FTS/rtree/json usage (if present)
- A mix of “clean” and “messy” schemas

Avoid / require explicit approval:
- DBs likely to contain secrets/PII
- Huge DBs that are impractical to store/copy locally

## Process

1. Use `realdb-e2e corpus scan --root /dp` to discover candidates.
2. Apply the inclusion policy (size + PII guardrails).
3. Import via backup API (`bd-2als.1.1`) and generate metadata.
4. Tag fixtures (size bucket, features, journaling, page_size) so workloads can select them intentionally.

## Output

- New golden DBs under `sample_sqlite_db_files/golden/` (local-only)
- Updated `metadata/*.json` + `checksums.sha256` (+ manifest if used)","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- At least 5 additional fixtures are ingested from `/dp` (or other approved sources).
- Each fixture has metadata with tags sufficient for fixture selection (size/features/journal/page_size).
- `realdb-e2e corpus verify` reports OK for all fixtures.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:13.463288932Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T20:10:02.183255415Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T20:10:02.183233394Z","close_reason":"Corpus expanded with fixtures: northwind, fix_my_documents_backend, beads_rust_beads (beads w/ WAL sidecars), eidetic_memory (FTS). Metadata tags include size bucket + wal/fts/many-indexes + page-size; corpus verify is clean.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","fixtures","realdb"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2als.1.2","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:13.463288932Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2als.1.2","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:23:10.153078569Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2als.1.2","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.1.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:23:10.385446896Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1204,"issue_id":"bd-2als.1.2","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Imported additional fixtures into corpus (metadata+checksums only; golden .db bytes remain git-ignored):

@@ -368,12 +368,12 @@
 {"id":"bd-2als.2.2","title":"Perf Runner: execute matrix (warmup+repeats) and write BenchmarkSummary JSONL","description":"## Goal

Implement the benchmark runner that executes the perf matrix and writes stable artifacts.

This is the “engine” behind:

- `realdb-e2e bench ...`

## Responsibilities

For each matrix cell (engine × fixture × workload × concurrency × repeat):

- Create a fresh working copy from the golden fixture.
- Apply fairness PRAGMAs.
- Run the workload.
- Record:
  - wall-clock duration
  - throughput
  - latency percentiles (if captured)
  - retry/busy/abort counters
  - any engine-specific metrics (MVCC conflicts, merge attempts)

Then aggregate into:

- `BenchmarkSummary` per (engine, fixture, workload, concurrency)

## Output

- JSONL file where each line is either:
  - a raw run record
  - or an aggregated summary record

- Stable output directory layout so other tools (report/TUI/CI) can find things.

## Constraints

- Must not mutate golden fixtures.
- Must handle sqlite3 busy gracefully (retry loop + counters).
- Must be deterministic at the “inputs” level (same seed/workload -> same operation stream).","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- Runner can execute a small matrix and emit JSONL records + summaries.
- Warmup + repeats are implemented as per spec.
- Output layout is stable and documented.
- sqlite3 busy/retry and fsqlite MVCC conflict metrics are recorded.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:41.837256998Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T17:41:17.442551842Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","perf","realdb","runner"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2als.2.2","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.1.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:23:36.255755259Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2als.2.2","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.2","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:41.837256998Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2als.2.2","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.2.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:23:35.973198209Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2als.2.2","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.3.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:23:36.540017675Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-2als.2.3","title":"Perf Report: render markdown (speedup curves, p95) + stable artifact paths","description":"## Goal

Turn benchmark records into a clear human-readable story:

- scaling curves vs concurrency
- speedup ratios vs sqlite3 baseline
- latency percentile comparisons
- context (fixture, workload, PRAGMAs, environment)

## Inputs

- JSONL run records and/or `BenchmarkSummary` aggregates produced by the perf runner.

## Output

- Markdown report(s), ideally with:
  - per-fixture sections
  - per-workload tables
  - speedup summaries
  - a short narrative that explains what the numbers mean

Optional:
- small inline ASCII sparklines / text graphs (keep it terminal-friendly)

## Critical Reporting Rules

- Always include the baseline settings (journal_mode, synchronous, cache_size, busy_timeout policy).
- Always include the seed and repeat count.
- Clearly label when sqlite3 results are “serialized via busy/retry” vs genuine parallelism.

## Why

Raw JSONL is great for machines; we need a report humans can read during a demo.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- A single command can render a Markdown report from a JSONL/summary directory.
- Report includes speedup ratios and scaling curves for at least one fixture/workload.
- Report includes enough context to be defensible (PRAGMAs, environment, seeds).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:41.893912520Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T17:41:31.862583342Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","perf","realdb","reporting"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2als.2.3","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.2","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:41.893912520Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2als.2.3","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.2.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:23:36.812921566Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-2als.2.4","title":"Concurrency Showcase: massively concurrent write workloads (sqlite3 vs fsqlite MVCC)","description":"## Goal

Build the flagship “massively concurrent writes” demo that makes FrankenSQLite’s MVCC advantage obvious.

This is not a micro-benchmark; it should feel like:

> “We threw 32–128 writer tasks at a real SQLite DB file, and FrankenSQLite finished in a fraction of the time while sqlite3 serialized.”

## Workload Design

We need at least two canonical cases:

1. **Disjoint writes (best case):** writers hit disjoint key ranges / tables such that conflicts are rare and MVCC scales.
2. **Hot-page contention (stress case):** writers collide on the same leaf/index page to show conflict behavior and merge/abort instrumentation.

## sqlite3 Baseline Policy

sqlite3 cannot truly run concurrent writers; it will busy/lock.
The baseline must:

- run in WAL mode where appropriate
- use a defined busy_timeout/retry strategy
- count retries and time spent waiting

We should present sqlite3 results honestly as “serialized under contention.”

## Presentation

Outputs should include:

- throughput / total time vs concurrency
- retry/busy metrics (sqlite3) and conflict/abort/merge metrics (fsqlite)
- a one-paragraph “what happened” explanation suitable for demos","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- A single command runs the showcase sweep (e.g., 1..64 writers) on a real fixture.
- Results clearly show scaling/speedup for disjoint-write case.
- Hot-page case produces useful conflict metrics and does not deadlock/hang.
- Artifacts (JSONL + Markdown) are generated in a stable output directory.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:42.172045841Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T17:41:45.620372405Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","mvcc","perf","realdb"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2als.2.4","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.2","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:42.172045841Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2als.2.4","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.2.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:23:37.098036289Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"bd-2als.3","title":"EPIC: Correctness Harness (Canonicalization + Tiered Compare + SHA256)","description":"## Goal

Make correctness verification against **real DB fixtures** routine and automated.

This epic defines how we:

- generate deterministic workloads (including concurrent writer patterns)
- run them against sqlite3 and fsqlite
- compare results under an explicit equivalence contract (Tier 1/2/3)
- produce mismatch artifacts that make failures debuggable

## Equivalence Tiers (Contract)

Because byte-identical DB files are a *very* strong requirement, we treat it as a tiered contract:

- Tier 1: raw sha256 equality of the final DB file (bit-for-bit)
- Tier 2: canonicalization pipeline produces identical canonical bytes (sha256)
- Tier 3: logical equivalence (schema + deterministic dump)

Workload presets must declare which tier they are expected to satisfy.

## Why This Is Critical

The whole RealDB effort relies on being able to say:

> “After these transformations, FrankenSQLite produced the same result as SQLite.”

If we can’t make that statement with strong evidence, perf numbers and corruption demos aren’t credible.

## What “Deterministic Workload” Means

- Fully seeded RNG (stable across runs)
- Stable operation ordering rules
- For concurrency: deterministic operation logs + schedule rules so we can replay and compare

## Outputs

- Canonicalization step (e.g., `VACUUM INTO`) to converge layout where possible.
- Comparison engine with rich diagnostics (pragma diffs, schema diffs, dump diffs).
- Mismatch repro bundles (minimal failing prefix + seed + fixture id + engine settings).","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- Workload presets have explicit tier expectations (Tier1/Tier2/Tier3).
- Canonicalization produces stable hashes for repeated runs.
- Batch runner can run a fixture/workload matrix and emit clear PASS/FAIL verdicts.
- Failures produce actionable artifacts (minimal repro, diffs, logs) rather than “hash mismatch” with no context.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:20:56.347628527Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T17:38:18.246145127Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["correctness","e2e","realdb","sha256"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2als.3","depends_on_id":"bd-2als","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:20:56.347628527Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2als.3","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:22:55.486018527Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-2als.3","title":"EPIC: Correctness Harness (Canonicalization + Tiered Compare + SHA256)","description":"## Goal

Make correctness verification against **real DB fixtures** routine and automated.

This epic defines how we:

- generate deterministic workloads (including concurrent writer patterns)
- run them against sqlite3 and fsqlite
- compare results under an explicit equivalence contract (Tier 1/2/3)
- produce mismatch artifacts that make failures debuggable

## Equivalence Tiers (Contract)

Because byte-identical DB files are a *very* strong requirement, we treat it as a tiered contract:

- Tier 1: raw sha256 equality of the final DB file (bit-for-bit)
- Tier 2: canonicalization pipeline produces identical canonical bytes (sha256)
- Tier 3: logical equivalence (schema + deterministic dump)

Workload presets must declare which tier they are expected to satisfy.

## Why This Is Critical

The whole RealDB effort relies on being able to say:

> “After these transformations, FrankenSQLite produced the same result as SQLite.”

If we can’t make that statement with strong evidence, perf numbers and corruption demos aren’t credible.

## What “Deterministic Workload” Means

- Fully seeded RNG (stable across runs)
- Stable operation ordering rules
- For concurrency: deterministic operation logs + schedule rules so we can replay and compare

## Outputs

- Canonicalization step (e.g., `VACUUM INTO`) to converge layout where possible.
- Comparison engine with rich diagnostics (pragma diffs, schema diffs, dump diffs).
- Mismatch repro bundles (minimal failing prefix + seed + fixture id + engine settings).","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- Workload presets have explicit tier expectations (Tier1/Tier2/Tier3).
- Canonicalization produces stable hashes for repeated runs.
- Batch runner can run a fixture/workload matrix and emit clear PASS/FAIL verdicts.
- Failures produce actionable artifacts (minimal repro, diffs, logs) rather than “hash mismatch” with no context.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:20:56.347628527Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T21:09:16.911529228Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["correctness","e2e","realdb","sha256"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2als.3","depends_on_id":"bd-2als","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:20:56.347628527Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2als.3","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:22:55.486018527Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-2als.3.1","title":"Workload Suite: deterministic transforms + concurrent write OpLogs (disjoint + hot-page)","description":"## Goal

Define a suite of **deterministic, replayable workloads** that can be applied to real fixtures.

Workloads must cover:

- single-writer transforms (migrations, bulk inserts, index builds)
- massively concurrent writer patterns
  - disjoint page writes (expected to scale under MVCC)
  - hot-page contention (expected conflicts; measures merge/abort behavior)

## Key Design: Oplog

Represent workloads as a deterministic operation log:

- seed + workload name -> exact sequence of operations
- operations are “SQL-level” or “intent-level” (not raw bytes)
- can be applied to either engine (sqlite3 or fsqlite)

For concurrency:

- define a deterministic assignment of operations to workers
- define retry behavior deterministically (especially for sqlite3 busy)
- record timing + retry counts as metrics, but correctness comparison uses the final state

## Workload Presets (Examples)

- `commutative_inserts`: many inserts of disjoint keys
- `hot_page_contention`: many inserts/updates that collide on the same index/leaf
- `mixed_read_write`: OLTP-ish mix of reads + writes
- `large_txn`: fewer but large transactions to stress checkpoint/GC
- `schema_migration`: DDL + backfill patterns

## Output

- A catalog of presets with documented expectations:
  - determinism inputs
  - expected equivalence tier (Tier1/2/3)
  - concurrency sweep defaults","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- Workload presets are deterministic: same seed -> identical oplog.
- Workload runner can apply oplogs to both sqlite3 and fsqlite engines.
- At least one preset explicitly targets disjoint-page concurrency and one targets hot-page contention.
- Presets declare an equivalence tier expectation.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:27.969025886Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T20:37:47.468530354Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T20:37:47.468475762Z","close_reason":"Workload suite implemented in fsqlite-e2e: OpLog format + preset library + preset_catalog (EquivalenceTier expectations), deterministic generator (workload.rs), and executors for sqlite3+fsqlite; tests cover determinism + preset structure.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["correctness","e2e","realdb","workload"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2als.3.1","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.1.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:23:21.270674678Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2als.3.1","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:27.969025886Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-2als.3.2","title":"Canonicalization + Tiered Compare: VACUUM INTO SHA256 + logical fallback + diagnostics","description":"## Goal

Implement the “equivalence oracle” for RealDB runs.

Given:
- original golden fixture
- a workload + seed
- two engine outputs (sqlite3 vs fsqlite)

we need to determine whether outputs are equivalent, and at what strength.

## Tiered Comparison

Tier 1: Raw byte identity
- sha256 of final DB bytes matches
- strongest signal; used for layout-deterministic workloads

Tier 2: Canonical identity
- Apply deterministic canonicalization (e.g., `VACUUM INTO`) to each output.
- Compare canonical sha256.
- Canonicalization rules must be documented and stable.

Tier 3: Logical equivalence
- Compare schema (sqlite_master) and a deterministic row dump.
- Dump formatting must be stable and deterministic.

## Canonicalization Details

Canonicalization should:

- minimize nondeterminism in freelist layout
- eliminate WAL/state sidecars differences
- produce stable bytes for stable logical content

Typical pipeline:
- `PRAGMA` set to stable values
- `VACUUM INTO <canonical_path>`
- hash canonical file

## Diagnostics

When mismatch occurs, emit:

- which tier failed
- sha256 values
- key PRAGMAs (page_size, encoding, user_version, application_id)
- schema diffs (sqlite_master)
- dump diffs (if Tier 3)
- pointers to artifact files

## Why

Without high-quality diagnostics, “hash mismatch” becomes a dead end.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- Canonicalization produces stable hashes when run repeatedly on the same DB.
- Tiered compare returns an explicit verdict: Tier1 match, Tier2 match, Tier3 match, or mismatch.
- Mismatch output includes actionable diagnostics + artifact paths.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:28.003843364Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T21:02:08.317319424Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T21:02:08.317299697Z","close_reason":"Implemented tiered comparison + canonicalization (VACUUM INTO SHA-256) + logical fallback and rich mismatch diagnostics in fsqlite-e2e (canonicalize.rs/comparison.rs/golden.rs) with tests + CLI wiring.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["correctness","e2e","realdb","sha256"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2als.3.2","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:28.003843364Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-2als.3.3","title":"Batch Runner: run workload matrix across fixtures (sqlite3 vs fsqlite) and assert Tier-1 hash","description":"## Goal

Provide a “push button” correctness runner that executes a workload matrix across fixtures and engines.

This is how we turn the RealDB suite into something we can run routinely (locally and in CI smoke).

## Responsibilities

For each (fixture × workload × concurrency × seed):

1. Create a fresh working copy.
2. Run workload on sqlite3 baseline.
3. Run workload on fsqlite.
4. Run tiered comparison (Tier1/2/3 as configured).
5. Write a structured run record (JSON/JSONL) + a human summary.

## Output

- `results.json` / `results.jsonl` containing per-case verdicts and hashes.
- A Markdown report summarizing pass/fail and pointing to artifacts.

## Failure Policy

- For CI smoke: fail fast on the first mismatch.
- For local runs: optionally continue and produce a full matrix report.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- Runner can execute a small matrix end-to-end and emit results artifacts.
- The same matrix run twice (same seeds) yields the same verdicts/hashes.
- Failures link to mismatch artifact bundles.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:28.022180067Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T21:04:16.051466556Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["correctness","e2e","realdb","runner"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2als.3.3","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.1.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:23:22.056739369Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2als.3.3","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:28.022180067Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2als.3.3","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.3.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:23:21.515049445Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2als.3.3","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.3.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:23:21.781288666Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-2als.3.4","title":"Mismatch Artifacts: minimal repro bundles + human-readable diff reports","description":"## Goal

When correctness mismatches happen, we need *rapid debugging leverage*.

This task defines the artifact bundle produced on mismatch so that a human (or another agent) can reproduce and diagnose without re-running the whole matrix.

## Bundle Contents (Minimum)

- Fixture id + sha256 of golden input
- Workload name + seed + concurrency
- Engine settings (PRAGMAs, MVCC toggle, durability toggle)

- The failing working copies (or instructions to regenerate them)
- Canonicalized DB outputs (Tier2)
- Deterministic dumps and schema snapshots (Tier3)

- A short `REPRO.md` containing copy-paste commands to reproduce
- Any diffs:
  - schema diff
  - dump diff
  - important PRAGMA diff

## Size Discipline

Artifact bundles can get big. Prefer:

- store canonical outputs + diffs
- optionally omit full working copies if they can be regenerated deterministically
- include hashes to verify integrity

## Why

Without repro bundles, we end up with “hash mismatch somewhere” and lose days.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- A mismatch automatically produces a bundle with `REPRO.md`, hashes, and diffs.
- A different machine/agent can reproduce the mismatch using the bundle and documented commands.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:28.512216863Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T17:40:50.665597357Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["correctness","e2e","realdb","reporting"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2als.3.4","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:28.512216863Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2als.3.4","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.3.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:23:22.330369119Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"bd-2als.4","title":"EPIC: Corruption + Recovery Demos (sqlite3 vs fsqlite, WAL-FEC)","description":"## Goal

Create dramatic, reproducible corruption + recovery demos on real fixtures:

- corrupt copies of real DBs deterministically
- measure and record what sqlite3 does (open fails, partial salvage, or silent data loss)
- demonstrate FrankenSQLite recovery (WAL-FEC / repair symbols) with evidence

## Why

SQLite has checksum chains in WAL but no built-in self-repair. Torn writes, bit flips, and file truncation can permanently destroy data or render the DB unreadable.

FrankenSQLite’s “raptorq everything” durability story must be demonstrated end-to-end: detect corruption, repair from redundant symbols, and prove the recovered DB matches an expected equivalence tier.

## Safety Rules

- Never corrupt `/dp` originals.
- Never corrupt `golden/` fixtures.
- Corrupt only fresh working copies created for the scenario.

## Proof Bundle Requirements

Every scenario run must emit a bundle containing:

- fixture id + source sha256 + working copy sha256
- scenario name + parameters + seed
- a precise description of which bytes/pages/frames were modified
- sqlite3 outcome (exit code, error string, integrity_check output, dump tier outcomes)
- fsqlite outcome (recovery attempts, repaired symbols, post-repair checks, equivalence verdict)

## Outputs

- Scenario catalog suitable for CI smoke (small subset) and local “shock demo” runs.
- A runner that produces both JSON (machine) and narrative Markdown (human) outputs.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- Catalog includes multiple deterministic strategies (page zero/bitflip/header damage/WAL truncation/torn write simulation).
- Runner can execute: corrupt -> sqlite3 baseline -> fsqlite recovery -> verdict.
- At least one real fixture demonstrates a clear “sqlite3 loses / fsqlite recovers” story with artifacts.
- Outputs are stable and easy to browse (paths, filenames, summary index).","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:20:56.384549476Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T17:38:31.870309286Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["corruption","e2e","realdb","recovery"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2als.4","depends_on_id":"bd-2als","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:20:56.384549476Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2als.4","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:22:56.017483248Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"bd-2als.4","title":"EPIC: Corruption + Recovery Demos (sqlite3 vs fsqlite, WAL-FEC)","description":"## Goal

Create dramatic, reproducible corruption + recovery demos on real fixtures:

- corrupt copies of real DBs deterministically
- measure and record what sqlite3 does (open fails, partial salvage, or silent data loss)
- demonstrate FrankenSQLite recovery (WAL-FEC / repair symbols) with evidence

## Why

SQLite has checksum chains in WAL but no built-in self-repair. Torn writes, bit flips, and file truncation can permanently destroy data or render the DB unreadable.

FrankenSQLite’s “raptorq everything” durability story must be demonstrated end-to-end: detect corruption, repair from redundant symbols, and prove the recovered DB matches an expected equivalence tier.

## Safety Rules

- Never corrupt `/dp` originals.
- Never corrupt `golden/` fixtures.
- Corrupt only fresh working copies created for the scenario.

## Proof Bundle Requirements

Every scenario run must emit a bundle containing:

- fixture id + source sha256 + working copy sha256
- scenario name + parameters + seed
- a precise description of which bytes/pages/frames were modified
- sqlite3 outcome (exit code, error string, integrity_check output, dump tier outcomes)
- fsqlite outcome (recovery attempts, repaired symbols, post-repair checks, equivalence verdict)

## Outputs

- Scenario catalog suitable for CI smoke (small subset) and local “shock demo” runs.
- A runner that produces both JSON (machine) and narrative Markdown (human) outputs.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- Catalog includes multiple deterministic strategies (page zero/bitflip/header damage/WAL truncation/torn write simulation).
- Runner can execute: corrupt -> sqlite3 baseline -> fsqlite recovery -> verdict.
- At least one real fixture demonstrates a clear “sqlite3 loses / fsqlite recovers” story with artifacts.
- Outputs are stable and easy to browse (paths, filenames, summary index).","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:20:56.384549476Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T21:09:18.431282325Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["corruption","e2e","realdb","recovery"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2als.4","depends_on_id":"bd-2als","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:20:56.384549476Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2als.4","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:22:56.017483248Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-2als.4.1","title":"Corruption Catalog: deterministic strategies (page flip, wal truncate, frame bitrot, torn-write)","description":"## Goal

Define a catalog of deterministic corruption strategies that we can apply to working copies.

This catalog is the “menu” for the corruption demo runner and for CI smoke.

## Strategy Requirements

- Deterministic: (strategy, params, seed) uniquely determines the corrupted bytes.
- Safe: operates only on a working copy (never golden/original).
- Introspectable: records what was modified (page numbers, offsets, byte ranges).

## Candidate Strategies

Database file corruption:
- `header_zero`: zero bytes in the 100-byte header
- `page_zero(pgno)`: zero an entire page
- `bitflip(count, region)`: flip N bits in a chosen region
- `truncate(bytes)`: truncate the DB file at specific offsets

WAL / sidecar corruption:
- `wal_truncate(frames)`
- `wal_bitrot(frame_range, flips)`
- “torn write” simulation: partial frame writes / partial page writes

(Exact list can evolve, but must stay deterministic.)

## Output

- Strategy implementations + structured report output describing modifications.
- A stable “scenario id” string usable in reports and run directories.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- Catalog includes at least 4 distinct strategies covering header/page/WAL/truncation style failures.
- Each strategy produces a structured report including what changed.
- Running the same strategy+seed twice produces identical corrupted bytes (sha256).","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:55.488916066Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T20:16:26.078148142Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["corruption","e2e","realdb"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2als.4.1","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:55.488916066Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-2als.4.2","title":"fsqlite Recovery: WAL-FEC repair + classification (recovered/partial/lost) + proof artifacts","description":"## Goal

Implement the FrankenSQLite-side recovery path used by corruption demos.

Given a corrupted working copy, FrankenSQLite should:

- detect corruption
- attempt repair using available redundancy (WAL-FEC / repair symbols)
- produce a recovered DB (or explicit classification if impossible)
- emit evidence/proofs of what was repaired

## Recovery Classification

Every recovery attempt should end in one of:

- `recovered`: DB is usable and passes integrity checks; meets target equivalence tier
- `partial`: DB opens but some data is unrecoverable; must report what was lost
- `lost`: DB cannot be recovered with available symbols

## Evidence Requirements

- What corruption was detected (checksums, decode failures)
- What was repaired (symbols used, pages/frames repaired)
- Post-repair checks:
  - `PRAGMA integrity_check`
  - tiered equivalence verdict vs an expected baseline (where defined)

## Practical Notes

- Recovery must be deterministic for a given corrupted input + symbol set.
- Recovery artifacts should be written to the per-run output dir.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- Recovery runner produces a structured report with classification + proofs.
- At least one catalog scenario + fixture yields a `recovered` outcome with evidence.
- Post-repair validation runs (integrity_check + tiered compare as appropriate).","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:55.521746430Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T17:42:10.010259999Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","raptorq","realdb","recovery"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2als.4.2","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.1.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:23:51.730435688Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2als.4.2","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:55.521746430Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2als.4.2","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.4.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:23:51.094572885Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-2als.4.3","title":"sqlite3 Baseline: measure/record data loss behavior under corruption (integrity_check + diff tiers)","description":"## Goal

Define and implement the sqlite3 baseline behavior measurement for corrupted DBs.

We want an honest, evidence-based answer to:

> “What happens to this DB under stock SQLite after corruption?”

## What To Record

For each corrupted copy:

- Can sqlite3 open it?
- Does `PRAGMA integrity_check` pass/fail? Capture stdout/stderr.
- Can we dump schema/data? If so, capture deterministic dump outputs.
- What error codes/messages occur?

## Tiered Outcome For sqlite3

Even if sqlite3 opens the DB, it may have lost data.
We should classify:

- `open_failed`
- `integrity_failed`
- `opened_but_logically_diverged` (dump differs vs expected)
- `opened_and_matches` (rare; corruption might be in unused region)

## Why

We need baseline evidence so the fsqlite recovery story is credible.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- For each scenario run, sqlite3 baseline results are recorded and summarized.
- Output includes integrity_check results and (when possible) a deterministic dump.
- At least one scenario demonstrates meaningful failure or data loss under sqlite3.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:55.601143058Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T17:42:20.494476740Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["corruption","e2e","realdb","sqlite3"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2als.4.3","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.1.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:23:52.199081267Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2als.4.3","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.4","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:21:55.601143058Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2als.4.3","depends_on_id":"bd-2als.4.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T17:23:51.397304161Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
